lessons.title: Hello & Goodbye
lessons.description: Learn how to greet people and say goodbye in different situations.

content.explanation: In English, we use different greetings depending on who we talk to.

items.note: Formal and informal — always safe to use

content.explanation: After greeting, we usually ask how the other person is feeling.
There are many ways to end a conversation in English.

items.note: When you will meet again soon
When you will meet next day

content.instruction: Listen to each phrase and repeat it aloud. Focus on natural rhythm.

content.question: You arrive at work at 9am and see your manager. What do you say?
content.explanation: 'Good morning' is correct — it's formal and appropriate before 12pm.

content.question: Someone says: 'How are you?' — What is the best reply?
content.explanation: 'I'm fine, thank you' is the standard polite reply to 'How are you?'

content.question: You meet someone for the very first time. What do you say at the end?
content.explanation: 'Nice to meet you' is used when meeting someone for the first time.

content.sentence: Good _____, Mr. Kumar. How are you?
content.explanation: 'Good morning' is the formal greeting used in the morning.

content.sentence: It was great meeting you! _____  to meet you too!
content.explanation: 'Nice to meet you too' is the reply when someone says 'Nice to meet you'.

content.instruction: Match each phrase on the left with the correct reply on the right.
Arrange the words to make a correct sentence.
content.explanation: This is the standard phrase when meeting someone for the first time.

content.scenario: It is 7pm. You walk into a shop and see the shopkeeper.
content.question: What is the most appropriate greeting?
content.explanation: After 6pm we say 'Good evening'. 'Good night' is only used when leaving at night or going to sleep.

content.instruction: Speak aloud! Imagine you are meeting a new colleague at work.
content.prompt: Greet them, ask how they are, and say goodbye.
content.example_response: Good morning! My name is Priya. How are you? It's nice to meet you! See you later. Goodbye!

tasks.title: Speaking practice — Reply to a greeting

content.instruction: Listen to this sentence and reply naturally, then say goodbye.
content.prompt: Someone says: 'Hi! How are you doing today?'
content.example_response: I'm doing great, thank you! How about you? Nice to meet you. Goodbye, see you later!

what_you_learned: Formal greetings: Hello, Good morning, Good afternoon, Good evening
Informal greetings: Hi, Hey, How's it going?
Asking how someone is: How are you? / How are you doing?
Replies: I'm fine, thanks / I'm great / Not bad
Goodbyes: Goodbye, Bye, See you later, Take care
First meeting: Nice to meet you / Nice to meet you too

lessons.title: Who am I? — Introducing yourself
lessons.description: Learn to introduce yourself with your name, country, age, and job.

content.explanation: These are the most important phrases to introduce yourself in English.

items.note: Tell people where you are from
Tell people where you live now

tasks.title: Grammar: verb 'to be' — am / is / are

content.explanation: The verb 'to be' changes depending on who we are talking about.
Common jobs you might use when introducing yourself.

content.instruction: Listen to this self-introduction and repeat each line aloud.

content.question: Complete the sentence: 'She ___ from Japan.'
content.explanation: We use 'is' with He / She / It.

content.question: Complete the sentence: 'They ___ teachers at the school.'
content.explanation: We use 'are' with We / You / They.

content.question: Which sentence is used to say where you come from?
content.explanation: 'I'm from ___' tells people your home country or city.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — verb to be

content.sentence: Hi! My name ___ Carlos and I ___ from Mexico.
content.explanation: 'name is' — we use 'is' because 'name' is third person. 'I am' — first person.

content.sentence: I ___ a nurse. I work at a hospital.
content.explanation: With 'I' we always use 'am'.

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct introduction sentence.
content.explanation: The structure is: My name + is + [your name].

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct sentence.
content.explanation: We say 'from [city] in [country]' to be more specific.

content.instruction: Match each subject with 'am', 'is', or 'are'.
This sentence has a grammar mistake. Choose the correct version.
content.wrong_sentence: I are from India and I is a student.
options: I is from India and I am a student.
I am from India and I am a student.
I am from India and I is a student.
I are from India and I are a student.
content.explanation: With 'I' we always use 'am'. Never 'is' or 'are' with 'I'.

content.instruction: Speak for 30–45 seconds. Introduce yourself completely.
content.prompt: Say your name, where you are from, how old you are, and what you do.
content.example_response: Hello! My name is Priya. I'm from Chennai, India. I'm 27 years old. I work as a software engineer. Nice to meet you!

content.instruction: Now introduce a friend or family member using 'He is' or 'She is'.
content.prompt: Talk about someone you know. Say their name, where they are from, and their job.
content.example_response: This is my friend Kavya. She's from Bangalore. She's 25 years old. She's a doctor.

what_you_learned: How to say your name: My name is ___ / I'm ___
How to say where you're from: I'm from ___
Verb 'to be': I am, You are, He/She/It is, We/They are
Short forms: I'm, You're, He's, She's, They're
Common jobs: teacher, doctor, engineer, student, nurse
How to introduce someone else: This is my friend ___. She is a ___.

lessons.title: Asking about others
lessons.description: Learn to ask basic questions about other people — name, age, job, and origin.

content.explanation: These are the most common question words in English.

items.note: Asking about a thing or name — What's your name?
Asking about a place — Where are you from?
Asking about a person — Who is she?
Asking about condition — How are you?
Asking about age — How old are you?

content.explanation: These questions are very common when you first meet someone.
To make a Yes/No question, put 'am / is / are' BEFORE the subject.
content.rule: Statement: Subject + am/is/are. Question: Am/Is/Are + Subject?

content.instruction: Listen to each question and repeat it with the correct intonation. Questions go UP at the end.

content.question: You want to know someone's job. Which question do you ask?
content.explanation: 'What do you do?' is the standard question to ask about someone's job or study.

content.question: You want to know where someone lives. Which question do you ask?
content.explanation: 'Where are you from?' asks about the place someone is from.

content.question: Turn this into a question: 'She is a nurse.'
content.explanation: Yes/No question: move 'is' to the front → Is + she + a nurse?

content.sentence: _____ are you from? — I'm from Lagos, Nigeria.
content.explanation: 'Where' asks about a place.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — Yes/No reply

content.sentence: Are you a teacher? — Yes, I _____.
content.explanation: Short answer to 'Are you...?' is 'Yes, I am.' Never 'Yes, I are.'

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — negative reply

content.sentence: Is he from France? — No, he _____.
content.explanation: Short negative: No, he isn't. (is + not = isn't)

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct question.
content.explanation: The structure is: How old + are + you?

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct Yes/No question.
content.explanation: Yes/No question: Are + you + a student?

content.instruction: Match each question with the correct answer.
Which sentence is grammatically correct?
content.explanation: In questions with 'to be', the verb comes before the subject: Where + are + you?

content.scenario: You're at a party. You want to know if the new person you just met is from your city.
content.question: What is the most natural question to ask?
content.explanation: 'Are you from Chennai?' is a Yes/No question. Natural and polite.

content.instruction: Imagine you are meeting a new person. Ask them 4 questions and answer them yourself, as if you are both sides of the conversation.
content.prompt: Practice this conversation: Ask their name, where they're from, their age, and their job.
content.example_response: Hi! What's your name? — My name is Sara. Where are you from? — I'm from Delhi. How old are you? — I'm 28. What do you do? — I work as a designer.

what_you_learned: Question words: What, Where, Who, How, How old
Questions: What's your name? / Where are you from? / How old are you? / What do you do?
Yes/No questions: move is/are to the front → Is she a nurse?
Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, I'm not. / Yes, she is. / No, he isn't.
Intonation: questions go UP at the end

lessons.title: People around me — family & friends
lessons.description: Learn vocabulary for family members and how to describe people using adjectives.

content.explanation: These are the most important words for describing your family.
Use these adjectives to describe people around you.
Possessive adjectives show who something belongs to.
content.rule: Possessive adjective + noun: my sister, his father, her mother

content.explanation: Use 'to be' + adjective to describe a person.

examples.sentence: His grandma is old but funny.

content.instruction: Repeat these sentences describing family members.

phrases.text: His brother is tall and funny.
Her grandmother is old but friendly.

content.question: What do you call your father's mother?
content.explanation: Your father's or mother's mother is your grandmother.

content.question: Complete: 'She has a brother. ___ brother is a doctor.'
content.explanation: 'She' → 'her'. We use 'her' for a female person.

content.question: Complete: 'I have two sisters. ___ sisters are very funny.'
content.explanation: The speaker is talking about their own sisters, so we use 'My'.

content.sentence: He has a wife. _____ wife is a nurse.
content.explanation: 'He' → 'His'. We use 'His' for a male person.

tasks.title: Fill in the correct family word

content.sentence: My father's sister is my _____.
content.explanation: Your father's or mother's sister is your aunt.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — adjective sentence

content.sentence: My brother is very _____ and he makes everyone laugh.
content.explanation: 'Funny' means someone who makes people laugh — matches 'makes everyone laugh'.

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct sentence.
content.explanation: Structure: My + [noun] + is + very + [adjective].

content.instruction: Match each description with the correct family word.
Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: She has a brother. His brother is a teacher.
options: She has a brother. His brother is a teacher.
She has a brother. Her brother is a teacher.
She has a brother. My brother is a teacher.
She has a brother. Our brother is a teacher.
content.explanation: We are talking about 'She', so we use 'Her brother', not 'His'.

content.scenario: You are showing a photo of your family to a new friend.
content.question: You point to your mother. What do you say?
options: This is her mother. She is kind.
This is my mother. She is kind.
This is my mother. He is kind.
This is our mother. Their is kind.
content.explanation: Use 'my' (I am speaking about my own mother) and 'She' (mother is female).

content.instruction: Describe 2–3 people in your family. Say their relationship, their job, and one adjective.
content.prompt: Tell me about someone in your family.
content.example_response: My mother is a teacher. She is very kind and hardworking. My brother is a student. He is young and funny. My father is an engineer. He is tall and very smart.

what_you_learned: Family words: mother, father, brother, sister, husband, wife, son, daughter, grandparents
Adjectives: tall, short, old, young, kind, friendly, funny, smart, hardworking
Possessives: my, your, his, her, our, their
Rule: He → His, She → Her, I → My
Structure: My [family member] is [adjective].

lessons.title: Where I live — places & locations
lessons.description: Learn vocabulary for places in your area and how to describe your neighbourhood.

content.explanation: Learn the different words for where people live.

items.note: A building where a family lives — usually has a garden
A home inside a large building — many floors
A large urban area with many people

content.explanation: These are common places you find in a city or town.

items.note: Open green area for relaxing and walking
Where you buy food and things
Where sick people go for treatment

content.explanation: We use 'There is' for one thing and 'There are' for many things.

examples.sentence: There is a park near my house.
There are many shops on my street.
There isn't a school in my village.

content.rule: There is + singular noun. There are + plural noun.

content.explanation: Use these words to describe WHERE something is.

items.note: Not far — There is a park near my house.
Not close — The school is far from here.
Beside — The bank is next to the market.
Directly across — The park is opposite my building.
Inside a place — I live in Chennai.
On a street — I live on MG Road.

content.instruction: Repeat these sentences about your neighbourhood.

phrases.text: I live in a flat in the city.
There is a park near my home.
There are many restaurants on my street.
The hospital is next to the school.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence: 'There ___ a supermarket near my house.'
content.explanation: 'A supermarket' is singular → use 'There is'.

content.question: '___ many parks in this city.'
content.explanation: 'Many parks' is plural → use 'There are'.

content.question: Where do you go if you need to buy medicine?
content.explanation: A pharmacy is a shop where you buy medicine.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — There is / are

content.sentence: There _____ three parks in my neighbourhood.
content.explanation: 'Three parks' is plural — use 'There are'.

content.sentence: I live _____ a small apartment _____ the city centre.
content.explanation: We use 'in' for buildings and for areas/cities.

content.sentence: The pharmacy is _____ the hospital — they are right beside each other.
content.explanation: 'Next to' means beside something — right next to it.

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct sentence.
content.correct_sentence: There is a school near my house.
content.explanation: Structure: There is + a + [noun] + [location word] + my house.

tasks.title: Match the place to its description

content.instruction: Match each place with what you do there.

pairs.answer: get treatment when you are sick

content.instruction: Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: There are a park near my house.
options: There are a park near my house.
There is a park near my house.
There am a park near my house.
There is parks near my house.
content.explanation: 'A park' is singular → 'There is'. Plural 'parks' (no article) → 'There are parks'.

content.scenario: A new friend asks you: 'Is your area a good place to live?'
content.question: Which is the best reply?
options: Yes, there is many shops and a big park near my home.
Yes, there are many shops and a big park near my home.
Yes, there are many shops and a big park near my home. It's very convenient.
Yes, it have shops and park.
content.explanation: 'There are many shops' (plural) + adding extra detail makes it the best, most natural reply.

content.instruction: Speak for 45–60 seconds about where you live.
content.prompt: Describe your home and neighbourhood. Use 'There is / There are' and location words.
content.example_response: I live in an apartment in Chennai. It's in a busy area. There is a supermarket near my home and there are two parks close by. There is a school opposite my building. It's a great neighbourhood — everything is close!

what_you_learned: Home types: house, apartment/flat, room
Area types: city, town, village
Places: park, market, hospital, school, restaurant, pharmacy, bank
There is + singular / There are + plural
Negative: There isn't / There aren't
Location words: near, next to, opposite, far from, in, on

lessons.title: My day — daily routines
lessons.description: Learn to talk about what you do every day using the simple present tense.

content.explanation: These action words (verbs) describe what we do every day.

items.note: Stop sleeping and open your eyes
Travel to your workplace or school

items.phrase: go to sleep / go to bed

content.explanation: Use these words to say WHEN you do things.
We use the simple present to talk about habits and routines — things we do regularly.

negative_form.i_you_we_they: do not (don't) + verb — I don't cook every day.
negative_form.he_she_it: does not (doesn't) + verb — She doesn't wake up early.

content.instruction: Listen and repeat these routine sentences.

phrases.text: I wake up at seven in the morning.
She goes to work at nine o'clock.
He doesn't cook — he eats at a restaurant.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence about your friend Sara:
options: Sara wake up at 6 every morning.
Sara wakes up at 6 every morning.
Sara is wake up at 6 every morning.
Sara waking up at 6 every morning.
content.explanation: He/She/It: add -s to the verb. wake → wakes.

content.question: 'He ___ watch TV in the morning.' (negative)
content.explanation: He/She/It negative → doesn't + base verb. Never 'don't' with He/She.

content.question: You eat breakfast at 8am. When do you eat?
content.explanation: 8am is in the morning — before 12pm.

tasks.title: Fill in the correct verb form

content.sentence: My father _____ (wake) up at 5am every day.
content.explanation: 'My father' = He → add -s. wake → wakes.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — exception verb

content.sentence: She _____ (have) lunch at 1pm at the office.
content.explanation: 'Have' is irregular — He/She/It has (not 'haves').

content.sentence: I _____ cook in the morning. I always have breakfast outside.
content.explanation: 'I' → negative is 'don't'. She/He → 'doesn't'.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct daily routine sentence.
content.correct_sentence: She goes to school in the morning.
content.explanation: She + goes (He/She form) + to school + time expression.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct negative sentence.
content.explanation: He/She/It negative: Subject + doesn't + base verb.

tasks.title: Match the routine to the time

content.instruction: Match each activity with the most likely time of day.
Which sentence is grammatically correct?
content.wrong_sentence: My brother go to the gym every morning.
options: My brother go to the gym every morning.
My brother goes to the gym every morning.
My brother going to the gym every morning.
My brother does go to the gym every morning.
content.explanation: 'My brother' = He → go + es = goes. He goes to the gym.

content.scenario: A colleague asks you: 'What time do you usually have lunch?'
content.question: Which is the most natural and correct reply?
options: I usually have lunch at 1 o'clock.
I usually having lunch at 1 o'clock.
content.explanation: Frequency adverb 'usually' goes before the main verb. 'I usually have...' is correct.

content.instruction: Describe your typical day from morning to night. Use time words and frequency words.
content.prompt: Tell me about your daily routine. What do you do every day?
content.example_response: I wake up at 6 in the morning. I have breakfast at 6:30. Then I go to work at 8. I usually have lunch at 1pm. I come home at 6 in the evening. I cook dinner and watch TV. I go to sleep at 10:30 at night.

content.instruction: Now talk about a family member's routine. Use He/She + verb+s.
content.prompt: Describe a typical day for your mother, father, or a friend.
content.example_response: My mother wakes up very early — at 5am. She makes breakfast for the family. She goes to work at 8. She usually has lunch at the office. She comes home at 6 in the evening and cooks dinner. She goes to sleep at 10.

what_you_learned: Daily verbs: wake up, have breakfast, go to work, come home, cook, watch TV, go to sleep
Time words: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening, at night, every day
Simple present: I wake / She wakes / He goes / She has
Negative: I don't cook / She doesn't wake up early
Frequency words: always, usually, sometimes
He/She exceptions: go → goes, have → has, watch → watches

lessons.title: What I like — hobbies & preferences
lessons.description: Learn to talk about your hobbies and express what you like or dislike.

content.explanation: These are popular hobbies and things people enjoy in their free time.
We use 'like', 'love', 'enjoy', and 'hate' followed by the verb + -ing form.
content.rule: Most verbs: add -ing. Verbs ending in -e: remove -e, add -ing.

content.explanation: Use these phrases to ask and answer about what people like.

examples.question: What do you like doing in your free time?
examples.yes: I like reading and listening to music.

examples.question: Do you enjoy travelling?
examples.yes: Yes, I enjoy it very much.
examples.no: Not really. I prefer staying at home.

content.instruction: Listen and repeat these sentences about hobbies.

phrases.text: What do you like doing at the weekend?

content.question: Complete: 'I enjoy _____ to new places.'
content.explanation: After 'enjoy', always use verb + -ing. travel → travelling.

content.question: Complete: 'She loves _____ movies on weekends.'
content.explanation: After 'loves', always use verb + -ing. watch → watching.

content.question: Which sentence means: 'Shopping is not something I enjoy.'
content.explanation: To express a negative preference: I don't like + verb-ing.

content.sentence: I love _____ (listen) to music when I come home from work.
content.explanation: After 'love', use verb + -ing. listen → listening.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — -ing form rule

content.sentence: He enjoys _____ (hike) in the mountains on weekends.
content.explanation: 'hike' ends in -e, so remove -e and add -ing → hiking.

tasks.title: Fill in the blank — negative preference

content.sentence: My sister _____ (not like) cooking. She prefers eating out.
content.explanation: 'My sister' = She → doesn't + base verb (no -s on like).

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a correct sentence.
content.explanation: I + love + verb-ing + and + verb-ing.

content.instruction: Rearrange the words to make a question about hobbies.
content.correct_sentence: Do you like cooking or enjoy it?
content.alternative_correct: Do you like or enjoy cooking?
content.explanation: 'Do you like cooking?' is the standard question form.

content.instruction: Match each verb with its correct -ing form.
Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: She likes to listen music every evening.
options: She likes to listen music every evening.
She likes listen to music every evening.
She likes listening to music every evening.
She like listening to music every evening.
content.explanation: After 'likes', use verb-ing. Also: 'listen TO music' (not 'listen music').

content.scenario: At a new job, a colleague asks: 'What do you do in your free time?'
content.question: Which is the most natural reply?
options: I am liking to read and travel.
I like reading and I enjoy travelling.
Reading and travel is what I like do.
content.explanation: 'I like reading and I enjoy travelling' — both use verb + -ing correctly.

content.instruction: Speak for 40–50 seconds about your hobbies and preferences.
content.prompt: What do you like doing in your free time? What do you not enjoy?
content.example_response: In my free time, I love listening to music and watching movies. I also enjoy cooking on weekends — I like trying new recipes. I don't like shopping very much. I hate waking up early on weekends! I prefer staying home and reading a good book.

content.instruction: Imagine you are asking a friend about their hobbies. Ask 3 questions and give example answers.
content.prompt: Ask a friend about what they like doing and what they don't enjoy.
content.example_response: Do you like cooking? — Yes, I love it! Do you enjoy hiking? — Not really. I prefer staying indoors. What do you like doing at weekends? — I love playing chess and listening to music.

what_you_learned: Hobby vocabulary: cooking, reading, travelling, playing sports, listening to music
Grammar: like / love / enjoy / hate + verb-ing
Negative: I don't like + verb-ing / She doesn't like + verb-ing
-ing form rules: add -ing (cook→cooking), drop -e (hike→hiking)
Questions: Do you like ___ing? / What do you enjoy doing?
Replies: Yes, I love it! / Not really. / I prefer ___.
content.next_lesson_title: Unit 1 Review — putting it all together

lessons.title: Unit 1 Review — putting it all together
lessons.description: Review everything from Unit 1 and prove you can talk about yourself, your family, your home, and your daily life.

content.explanation: Let's review all 7 lessons before your final test.

summary.topic: Greetings — Hello, How are you?, Goodbye
Introductions — My name is, I'm from, I'm a ___
Questions — What, Where, How old, Are you...?
Family — mother, father, sister + my/his/her + adjectives
Places — house, city, There is/are, near/next to
Daily routines — wake up, go to work + simple present

content.question: It's 8pm. You meet your neighbour at the door. What do you say?
content.explanation: 8pm = evening → 'Good evening!' (Good night is only used when leaving at night to go to sleep.)

content.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I are a student from Chennai.
She am a doctor at the hospital.
They is my friends from college.
He is a teacher at the school.
content.explanation: He → is. I → am. She → is. They → are.

content.question: Fill in the blank: 'She has a daughter. _____ daughter is 5 years old.'
content.explanation: She → Her. 'Her daughter' — the daughter belongs to 'she'.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: There are a bank next to the school.
There is many restaurants on my street.
There is a pharmacy near my home.
There am a park in this city.
content.explanation: 'A pharmacy' = singular → There is. 'Many restaurants' = plural → There are.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence about your friend:
options: My friend go to work at 8 every day.
My friend going to work at 8 every day.
My friend goes to work at 8 every day.
My friend is go to work at 8 every day.
content.explanation: 'My friend' = He/She → go + es = goes. Simple present, third person.

content.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I love to cook and travel.
content.explanation: After love/like/hate: use verb + -ing. cooking ✓, travelling ✓.

content.sentence: _____ name is Meera and I _____ from Coimbatore.
content.explanation: My name (possessive). I am (first person verb).

content.sentence: There _____ two parks and _____ a hospital near my home.
content.explanation: 'Two parks' = plural → are. 'A hospital' = singular → there is.

content.sentence: She _____ (wake) up at 6am and _____ (have) breakfast before work.
content.explanation: She → wakes (wake+s), has (irregular: have→has).

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a complete, correct sentence.
content.correct_sentence: My sister is very tall and kind.
content.explanation: My + [noun] + is + very + [adjective] + and + [adjective].

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct question.
content.correct_sentence: What do you do in your free time?
content.explanation: What + do + you + do + in your free time?

content.instruction: This paragraph has 3 grammar mistakes. Choose the corrected version.
content.wrong_text: My name are Ravi. I is from Delhi. I have a sister — her name is Priya. She are a doctor.
options: My name is Ravi. I am from Delhi. I have a sister — her name is Priya. She is a doctor.
My name is Ravi. I is from Delhi. I have a sister — her name is Priya. She are a doctor.
My name are Ravi. I am from Delhi. I have a sister — her name are Priya. She is a doctor.
My name is Ravi. I are from Delhi. I have a sister — her name is Priya. She am a doctor.
content.explanation: name IS (not are), I AM (not is), She IS (not are). All three are 'to be' mistakes.

content.instruction: Match each sentence with its grammar rule.

content.scenario: You are at an English class for the first time. The teacher asks you to introduce yourself.
content.question: Which introduction is the most complete and grammatically correct?
options: I Priya. From Chennai. Student here.
My name Priya. I am Chennai. I student.
Hello! My name is Priya. I'm from Chennai. I'm 24 years old and I work as a graphic designer. I love drawing and travelling.
Hello! I are Priya from Chennai. I is 24. I loving drawing.
content.explanation: Option C uses correct grammar throughout: My name is, I'm from, I'm 24, I work as, I love + -ing.

tasks.title: Final speaking test — Part 1: About yourself

content.instruction: This is your Unit 1 speaking test. Speak for 60–90 seconds.
content.prompt: Introduce yourself completely. Include: your name, where you're from, your age, your job or study, one family member, where you live, your daily routine, and your hobbies.
content.example_response: Hello! My name is Arjun. I'm from Madurai, India. I'm 26 years old. I work as a software developer. I live in Chennai now — in a small apartment. There is a big park and a supermarket near my home. Every day I wake up at 7, have breakfast, and go to work at 9. In the evenings, I like cooking and listening to music. I love travelling too! I have a sister — her name is Divya. She is a teacher and she is very kind.
key_phrases: I'm ___ years old / I work as
I wake up at / I go to
My ___ is / She is / He is

tasks.title: Final speaking test — Part 2: Describe your neighbourhood

content.instruction: Speak for 30–45 seconds specifically about where you live.
content.prompt: Describe your home and your area. Use There is/are and location words.
content.example_response: I live in an apartment in Chennai. It's a nice area. There is a big park opposite my building — I go there in the evenings. There are many restaurants and shops near my home. There is a school next to my building. The hospital is a bit far, but there is a pharmacy close by. I really like my neighbourhood.
key_phrases: near / next to / opposite / far from

content.congratulations: You have finished Unit 1! You can now introduce yourself, talk about your family, describe where you live, talk about your daily routine, and express your hobbies — all in English.
all_grammar_covered: Verb 'to be': am / is / are + contractions
Possessive adjectives: my, your, his, her, our, their
There is / There are (positive, negative, question)
Simple present tense (I wake / She wakes)
Simple present negative (I don't / She doesn't)
all_vocabulary_covered: Daily routine verbs and time expressions
content.next_unit_title: Unit 2 — Shopping, food, and making requests
